Title: INOSITOL (1,3,4) TRIPHOSPHATE 5/6 KINASE1-dependent inositol polyphosphates regulate auxin responses in Arabidopsis

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native ITPK1 promoter (1,839-bp upstream fragment); endogenous/native
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Inositol (1,3,4) trisphosphate 5/6 kinase family; inositol phosphate kinase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: InsP6-to-5-InsP7 branch and broader InsP interconversion network
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for 5-InsP7 homeostasis in planta; supports auxin-related signaling
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: InsP6; InsP4a (unknown structural identity, likely corresponding to an accumulated InsP4 isomer in itpk1)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates InsP6 to generate 5-InsP7 in vitro/in planta context; also phosphorylates an accumulated InsP4 isomer
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms regulatory PP-InsP(s) that activate/fine-tune auxin receptor complex and auxin responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: 5-InsP7; inositol polyphosphates/pyrophosphates involved in auxin signaling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fine-tunes auxin perception and downstream developmental responses including primary root elongation, root hair formation, gravitropism, thermomorphogenesis, and leaf venation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedlings; shoots and roots (measured from whole seedlings and tissues in Arabidopsis)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes auxin sensitivity and normal auxin-related development by sustaining 5-InsP7 and proper InsP homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; genomic complementation with pITPK1:gITPK1-G3GFP; CE-ESI-MS and SAX-HPLC metabolite profiling; in vitro kinase assay; auxin-response phenotyping; co-immunoprecipitation; BiFC; receptor binding assay context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitous; high transcript levels detected in all tested tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ubiquitous; high transcript levels across tissues
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus (interaction with TIR1 detected in nucleus by BiFC)
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous IAA; NAA; elevated temperature (29°C); gravitropic reorientation; phosphate-replete and low-phosphate conditions
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling by SAX-HPLC and CE-ESI-MS; RT-qPCR expression analysis; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. itpk1 mutants showed short primary roots, altered leaf venation, impaired gravitropism, thermomorphogenesis, and reduced auxin sensitivity. 2. itpk1 seedlings had reduced 5-InsP7 and InsP5[1/3-OH] plus accumulation of specific InsP3/InsP4 isomers; all defects were rescued by genomic ITPK1 complementation. 3. ITPK1 physically interacts with TIR1, supporting localized generation of an auxin-receptor-active inositol polyphosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes constitutive phosphate starvation responses and phosphate over-accumulation under phosphate-sufficient conditions, although this is largely independent from the auxin phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to tune hormone sensitivity and nutrient-signaling crosstalk, but manipulation may perturb phosphate homeostasis and development; careful context-specific engineering required
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No detectable change in bulk InsP6 in itpk1; reduced 5-InsP7 rather than InsP8 best correlated with auxin defects. Authors infer that 5-InsP7 or another ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP activates auxin receptor complex.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Inositol (1,3,4) trisphosphate 5/6 kinase family; inositol phosphate kinase
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream to downstream; partially redundant PP-InsP-forming step
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established alone; redundant contributor with ITPK1 for auxin responses
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Likely 5-InsP7 in vitro; exact in planta product contribution under tested conditions not resolved
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Displays InsP6 kinase activity in vitro similar to ITPK1 and redundantly contributes to auxin-related signaling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies redundant regulatory PP-InsP-generating capacity affecting auxin responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Inositol pyrophosphates linked to auxin signaling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports normal auxin-related root hair and gravitropic responses redundantly with ITPK1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts redundantly with ITPK1 to support auxin responses; single mutant shows no obvious phenotype but double mutant enhances auxin defects
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Single- and double-mutant phenotyping; RT-qPCR expression analysis; prior in vitro kinase activity cited; SAX-HPLC/CE-ESI-MS context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitous; notably strong in root and hypocotyl
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: SAX-HPLC and previously reported CE-ESI-MS; RT-qPCR; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ITPK2 is expressed broadly, especially in root and hypocotyl. 2. itpk2-2 single mutants showed no obvious InsP/PP-InsP or auxin-response phenotype under tested conditions. 3. itpk1 itpk2-2 double mutants displayed stronger root hair and gravitropism defects than itpk1, demonstrating functional redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be co-targeted with ITPK1 to modulate auxin sensitivity, but redundancy and possible broader inositol phosphate effects require caution
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In planta biochemical contribution appears masked in single mutant under Pi-replete conditions despite in vitro InsP6 kinase activity.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream step producing InsP6 precursor pool for PP-InsP synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key step for InsP6 and downstream PP-InsP homeostasis; not explicitly rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts InsP5[2-OH] to InsP6, thereby enabling downstream InsP7 and InsP8 formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for auxin-relevant inositol polyphosphates/pyrophosphates
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: InsP6-derived PP-InsPs involved in auxin signaling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports proper auxin perception by maintaining InsP6, InsP7, and InsP8 homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ipk1 mutants show decreased auxin responses associated with reduced InsP6 and PP-InsPs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; CE-ESI-MS profiling; prior biochemical assignment cited
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: CE-ESI-MS metabolite profiling; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ipk1-1 plants were compromised in auxin perception similar to itpk1. 2. ipk1 mutants had reduced InsP6, 5-InsP7, 1/3-InsP7, and 4/6-InsP7, plus altered lower InsPs. 3. Shared defects between ipk1 and itpk1 suggest that decreased 5-InsP7 and/or related InsPs are important for auxin signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point for altering inositol phosphate signaling, but likely broad pleiotropic effects due to central role in phytic acid/PP-InsP metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core pathway gene because Results directly tested ipk1 mutant auxin phenotypes and linked them to metabolite changes.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: F-box auxin receptor; LRR-containing SCF receptor component
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Auxin perception and downstream transcriptional response modulated by inositol polyphosphate cofactors
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Ligand-dependent co-receptor assembly with Aux/IAA and inositol polyphosphates; promotes auxin signaling
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Aux/IAA repressors including IAA7; auxin-responsive gene expression program
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes degradation/inactivation of Aux/IAA repressors, enabling auxin-responsive gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as the auxin receptor complex component whose activity is proposed to be fine-tuned by 5-InsP7 or another ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: IAA (auxin); ITPK1-dependent inositol polyphosphates such as 5-InsP7; ASK1 association
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: SCF^TIR1/ASK1 auxin receptor complex with ASK1 and Aux/IAA proteins such as IAA7
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: In vitro radioligand binding; co-IP; BiFC; no accession reported
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Inositol polyphosphates/pyrophosphates influencing auxin receptor activity
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fine-tuning of auxin perception and developmental responses
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: High-affinity binding partner for InsP6 and 5-InsP7 in auxin receptor complex; likely activated by ITPK1-derived inositol polyphosphates to promote auxin responsiveness
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Radioligand competitive binding assay with ASK1-TIR1-IAA7 complex; co-immunoprecipitation; BiFC
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ASK1-TIR1-IAA7 receptor complex bound highly anionic inositol polyphosphates with high affinity, with InsP6 and 5-InsP7 showing strongest binding. 2. TIR1 physically interacted with ITPK1 in planta. 3. These data support localized production/action of ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP to activate the auxin receptor complex.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential receptor-side engineering target to modulate hormone sensitivity via cofactor binding, though likely pleiotropic across auxin-regulated development
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TIR1 protein stability was not compromised in itpk1; phenotype is attributed to receptor activation state rather than receptor abundance.
